channel.title: My New Hugo Site
channel.description: Recent content on My New Hugo Site

item.title: Failure as a Path to Growth
item.description:
Reflection

The third unit involved creating a failure wiki in groups. This unit helped me learn a differernt perspective on failure and its role in the learning process. Initially, I used to associate failure with negative outcomes, but this unit introduced me the concept of intelligent failure and how to make failure a roadmap to success. Our group was assigned the Ariane 5 case study, which highlighted how minor mistakes can lead to significant failures. It also helped me understand that multiple small issues results in a failure.

item.title: Exploring Image Processing and Optimization
item.description:
Reflection

In this unit, we explored how images can be understood and processed using numbers and mathematical ideas. One of the first topics was learning about pixels and how images are represented digitally. Before this unit, I never really thought about how images are stored or processed inside a computer. It was interesting to learn that images are actually made up of many pixels, and each pixels store numerical values that represent color or brightness.

item.title: From Concepts to Practice: Learning Information, DIKW model, Markdown and My first reflection blog
item.description:
Reflection

In the first unit of the course, we started by looking at different graphs and discussing what was wrong with them. This activity was interesting because it showed how easily graphs can mislead people if they are not designed carefully. Another interesting part of the unit was practicing how to draw inferences from datasets. Writing a single sentence to summarize the meaning of a dataset was harder than I expected, but it was useful because it required us to focus on most important information rather than getting lost in the details.

item.title: My First Post
item.description:
Hi, I am Khushi Sharma.

I am currently pursuing a degree in Information Science. This blog is part of my course, “Becoming a Reflective Informative Scientist “, where we are encouraged not just to learn concepts, but to think, question and reflect on how we learn. I am still exploring this field and trying to understand things step by step. Through this blog, I will share my learning experience, the challenges I face and how I grow during this course.

Ariane 5 and its failure

At 1985 Ministral conference, ESA (European Space Agency) decided to develop a ‘Future European Launcher’ to replace Ariane 4 and to participate in the space station being proposed for international collaboration by NASA. The Ariane 5 programme was approved at the 1987 Ministral conference. The mission plans included for 1995 Ariane 5 were: Low-Earth orbit, Geostationary and Sun-synchronous. Due to malfunction in the control software, the maiden flight of Ariane 5 (Flight 501) failed 37 seconds after the launch on June 4, 1996. The second test flight (502) on 30th October 1997 was a partial failure. The third test flight (503) on 21st October 1998 was a success. What had caused the failure in launch? Ariane 5 software developers reused code from the Ariane 4 which had been developed 10 years before. The code that caused the problems was in the Inertial Reference System. This was responsible for aligning the rockets before take-off. The developers had added a delay to account for the common inaccuracy of flight launch time, meaning the code was running when it shouldn’t have been. The Inertial Reference System was used to determine whether it was increasing or decreasing, and this is known as horizontal bias or BH value. This value was represented by a 64-bit floating variable. A 64-bit floating point can represent numbers from 10^ -383 to 10^ 384. A 16-bit signed integer can store numbers from -32,768 to 32,767 Problems began to occur when the software attempted to stuff this 64-bit variable into a 16-bit integer. For the first few seconds of flight, the rockets’s acceleration was low, so the conversion between these two values were succesful. However, as the rocket’s velocity increased, the 64-bit variable exceeded 65k, and became too-large to fit in a 16-bit variable. At this point, the processor encountered an opperand error and increased the BH variable with a diagnostic value. The backup Initial Reference System also failed due to the same error condition. At T+37, the BH variable had a diagnostic value from the processor, intended for debugging purposes only.

Overview

Ariane 5 Flight 501 – Quick Facts

Ariane 5 Flight 501
Date June 4, 1996
Agency ESA
Outcome Failure (37 seconds after launch)
Cause Software error (overflow)
Loss ~$370 million

The Ariane 5 was developed by the European Space Agency as part of a long-term effort to maintain Europe’s access to space. In the 1985 Ministeral Conference, ESA decided to create a more powerful launch vehicle than Ariane 4 and to supporrt future missions, including taking part in space stations proposed by NASA. The Ariane 5 was officialy aprroved at the 1987 Ministeral Conference. It was designed to carry heavy payloads into Low-Earth Orbit, Geostationary Orbit and Sun-synchronous Orbit.
